elements.sent-2411-001: The standard quadratic optimization problem (StQP) consists of minimizing a quadratic form over the standard simplex.
elements.sent-2411-002: Without convexity or concavity of the quadratic form, the StQP is NP-hard.
elements.sent-2411-003: This problem has many relevant real-life applications ranging from portfolio optimization to pairwise clustering and replicator dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-004: Sometimes, the data matrix is uncertain.
elements.sent-2411-005: We investigate models where the distribution of the data matrix is known but where both the StQP after realization of the data matrix and the here-and-now problem are indefinite.
elements.sent-2411-006: We test the performance of a chance-constrained epigraphic StQP to the uncertain StQP.
elements.sent-2411-007: Keywords: Stochastic optimization, Quadratic optimization, Chance constraints, Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble\newline MSC(2020) Classification: 90C20 (quadratic programming), 90C15 (stochastic programming), 90C26 (nonconvex programming, global optimization).
elements.sent-2411-008: The standard quadratic optimization problem consists of minimizing a quadratic form over the standard simplex \begin{equation}\label{StQP} \ell(\Qb):=\min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}\Qb\x\end{equation} where $\Qb \in \mathbb{R ^{n\times n}$ is a symmetric matrix, and $\Delta:= \{\x \in \mathbb{R ^n: \e^{\top}\x = 1, \x \geq \boldsymbol{0}\}$ is the standard simplex in $\mathbb{R ^n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Here $\e\in \mathbb{R ^n$ is the vector of all ones and ${ ^{\top}$ denotes transposition; $\Ib= {\rm Diag (\e)$ denotes the $n\times n$ identity matrix.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The objective function is already in general form since any general quadratic objective function $\x^{\top}\Ab\x + 2\cc^{\top}\x$ can be written in homogeneous form by defining the symmetric matrix $\Qb:= \Ab + \cc\e^{\top + \e\cc^{\top}$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Even though the StQP is simple - minimization of a quadratic function under linear constraints - it is NP-hard without assumptions on the definiteness of the matrix $\Qb$; observe that convex, but also concave instances are polynomially solvable, the latter even in closed form: $\ell(\Qb) = \min_{i \Qb_{ii}$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Note that $\ell(\Qb)\ge 0$ is possible even if $\Qb$ is not positive semi-definite.
elements.sent-2411-013: In fact, the condition $\ell(\Qb)\ge 0$ characterizes copositivity~\cite{ShakedMondererBermanBook2021 of $\Qb$, and follows if no entry of $\Qb$ is negative (as, e.g. in the instances $\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)}$ generated in Section~\ref{sec:numerical_experiments below).}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Irrespective of the sign of $\ell(\Qb)$, its calculation can be hard for indefinite instances: indeed, Motzkin and Straus \cite{motzkin-straus-1965 showed that the maximum clique problem, a well-known NP-hard problem, can be formulated as an StQP.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Hence, the StQP is often regarded as the simplest of hard problems \cite{bomze2018complexity since it contains the simplest non-convex objective function which is a quadratic form, and the simplest polytope as feasible set.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Still, the StQP is a very flexible optimization class that allows for modelling of diverse problems such as portfolio optimization problems \cite{markowitz-1952 , pairwise clustering \cite{pavan2003dominant and replicator dynamics \cite{bomze1998standard .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite of its continuous (in fact, quadratic) optimization nature, it also serves to model discrete problems like the maximum-clique problem as well, as witnessed by above references.
elements.sent-2411-018: The only data required to fully characterize an StQP is the data matrix $\Qb$.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, in many applications the matrix $\Qb$ is uncertain.
elements.sent-2411-020: StQPs with uncertain data have been explored in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-021: One of the most natural ways to deal with uncertain objective functions is the worst-case approach, where there is no information on the distribution of the data matrix $\Qb$ except for its support.
elements.sent-2411-022: Bomze et al. \cite{Bomz20a introduced the concept of a robust standard quadratic optimization problem, which they formulated as a minimax problem \begin{equation}\label{robust_StQP} \min_{\x \in \Delta} \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U} \,\x^{\top}(\Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \Ub)\x.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The uncertain matrix $\Qb$ consisted of a nominal part $\Qb^{\rm (nom)}$ and an uncertain additive perturbation $\Ub$.
elements.sent-2411-024: In their paper, the authors investigated various uncertainty sets and proved that the copositive relaxation gap is equal to the minimax gap.
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, they observed that the robust StQP \eqref{robust_StQP reduces to a deterministic StQP for many frequently used types of uncertainty sets $\mathcal U$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Passing from a robust to stochastic setting with known expectation, a natural alternative to get rid of the uncertainty is the here-and-now problem (random quantities are designated by a tilde sign) \begin{equation}\label{here-and-now_StQP} \min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \mathbb E [\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb\x] = \min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}\mathbb E [\widetilde \Qb]\x\end{equation} where the uncertain matrix $\widetilde \Qb$ is replaced by its expectation $\mathbb E [\widetilde \Qb]$.
elements.sent-2411-027: In this paper, we propose an alternative to the here-and-now problem.
elements.sent-2411-028: Bomze et al. \cite{bomze2022two investigated a two-stage setting where the principal submatrix was deterministic and the rest of the entries followed a known probability distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this paper, we will however assume that the full data matrix $\widetilde \Qb$ is stochastic with known distribution $\mathbb P$, in which case models with two stages are less meaningful.
elements.sent-2411-030: The purpose of this note is to introduce, apparently for the first time, chance constraints for this problem class by introduction of an epigraphic variable, and moreover, to present a deterministic equivalent StQP formulation under reasonable distributional assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-031: Moreover, we establish a close connection of our new model to robustness with Frobenius ball uncertainty sets.
elements.sent-2411-032: It is well known that any optimization problem \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \mathcal{X}} \, f(\x)\end{equation} can be rewritten equivalently in epigraphic form by introducing an extra variable $t$ \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\underset{\x,t}{\min} \quad t \nonumber s.t. \quad f(\x) \leq t \label{soft_constraint} \quad \x \in X, \, t\in R. \label{hard_constraint}\end{equation} \end{subequations Now suppose that $f:\mathbb R^n \times \mathbb R^p \to \mathbb R$ is a random objective function that depends on the decision variable $\x \in \mathcal X \subseteq \mathbb R^n$ and on the random vector $\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi \in \Omega \subseteq \mathbb R^p$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\underset{\x,t}{\min} \quad t \nonumber s.t. \quad f(\x) \leq t \label{soft_constraint} \quad \x \in X, \, t\in R. \label{hard_constraint}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-033: For every fixed $\x \in \mathcal X$, $f(\x,\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi )$ is a random variable with a distribution $\mathbb P$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Then we can regard constraint \eqref{soft_constraint as a soft constraint and request feasibility with probability of at least $\alpha$ to obtain \eqref{prob_min_var .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Constraint \eqref{hard_constraint remains a hard constraint and must be satisfied for all feasible points.}
elements.sent-2411-036: This has a familiar interpretation familiar in risk management as the so-called value-at-risk (VaR) of $f(\x,\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi )$ at $\x$ with confidence level $\alpha \in (0,1)$, defined as~\cite{linsmeier1996risk \begin{equation}\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(\x):=\min \, \{ t: \mathbb P[f(\x,\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi}) \leq t] \geq \alpha\}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The problem of minimizing VaR~\cite{Larsen2002 is \begin{equation}\label{prob_min_var} \begin{array}{lcl} \underset{\x \in \mathcal X}{\min}\,\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(\x) = \underset{\x,t}{\min} t \text{s.t.} \mathbb P[f(\x,\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi}) \leq t] \geq \alpha \x \in \mathcal X, \, t\in \mathbb{R}\, . \end{array}\end{equation} El Ghaoui et al. \cite{ghaoui2003worst investigated problem~\eqref{prob_min_var in the context of worst-case VaR for portfolio optimization with a return function \begin{equation}f(\x,\tilde \xi) := \x^{\top}\tilde \xi\end{equation} where $\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi}$ was a vector of returns.}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{prob_min_var} {lcl} \underset{\x \in \mathcal X}{\min}\,VaR_{\alpha}(\x) = \underset{\x,t}{\min} t s.t. \mathbb P[f(\x,\tilde \xi) \leq t] \geq \alpha \x \in \mathcal X, \, t\in R\, . \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: Inspired by the VaR and passing from linear to quadratic expressions in the decision variable $\x$, we want to find the smallest number $t \in \mathbb R$ such that $\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb\x \leq t$ with probability at least $\alpha$, where $\alpha \in (0,1)$ is a confidence level provided by the decision-maker.
elements.sent-2411-039: This means solving the following stochastic optimization problem which we call chance-constrained epigraphic StQP \begin{equation}\label{StQP_cce}\tag{CCE-StQP} \begin{array}{lcl} \ell^{\rm (cce)}_{\mathbb P, \alpha} := \underset{\x,t}{\min} t \text{s.t.} \mathbb P(\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb\x \leq t) \geq \alpha \x \in \Delta\, ,\, t\in \mathbb{R}\, . \end{array}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\label{StQP_cce}\tag{CCE-StQP} {lcl} \ell^{\rm (cce)}_{\mathbb P, \alpha} := \underset{\x,t}{\min} t s.t. \mathbb P(\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb\x \leq t) \geq \alpha \x \in \Delta\, ,\, t\in R\, . \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-040: Two common and simple ways of generating an indefinite random symmetric matrix $\widetilde \Qb$ are:.
elements.sent-2411-041: Generate a random $n\times n$ matrix $\widetilde \Rb$ and put $\widetilde\Qb :=\Qb^{\rm (nom) + \frac{\beta {\sqrt{2 (\widetilde \Rb+\widetilde\Rb^{\top )$, for some nominal matrix $\Qb^{\rm (nom)}$ and parameter $\beta$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Generate a random $r\times n$ matrix $\widetilde \Fb$ and put $\widetilde \Qb := \widetilde\Fb^{\top}\widetilde\Fb$.
elements.sent-2411-043: This generates a convex instance, but it can be avoided by subtracting $\eta\,\Ib$ for large enough $\eta >0$, but not too large to avoid concave instances.
elements.sent-2411-044: In case that $\widetilde \Rb$ or $\widetilde \Fb$ are normal with i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-045: More generally, suppose that $\mathbb P$ enjoys the following property: \begin{property For all $\x\in \Delta$, the distribution of $\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb \x$ is of location/scale type where for some continuous cdf $F$ which is strictly increasing on $\{ t\in \mathbb R : F(t)>0\}$ \begin{equation}\mathbb P(\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb \x \leq t) = F\left(\frac{t - \mu(\x)}{\sigma(\x)}\right)\end{equation} with two parameters depending on $\x$ in the following way: \begin{equation}\mu(\x) = \x^{\top}\Mb\x, \quad \sigma(\x) = \x^{\top}\Sb\x > 0,\end{equation} where $\Mb$ and $\Sb$ are symmetric $n\times n$ matrices.. \end{property.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Property \ref{property is satisfied for both GOE (with $\Mb = \Qb^{\rm (nom) , \, \Sb = \beta\,\Ib$ and $F=\Phi$, see Corollary~\ref{propgoe below) and shifted Wishart (with $\Mb = -\eta\,\Ib, \Sb = 2\sSigma$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Indeed, for $\widetilde \Qb := \widetilde \Wb - \eta\, \Ib$ with Wishart matrix $\widetilde \Wb\sim \mathcal{W _n(\sSigma,p)$ we have that $\x^{\top}\widetilde\Wb\x$ is a gamma distributed random variable with shape $k= p/2$ and scale $\theta= 2\x^{\top}\sSigma\x$, where $p$ determines the shape of $F$.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Then the CCE-StQP can be reformulated as a deterministic StQP.
elements.sent-2411-049: Under Property \ref{property , we get for $\overline \Qb:=\Mb + F^{-1 (\alpha)\Sb$ \begin{equation}\ell(\overline \Qb) = \ell^{\rm (cce)}_{\mathbb P, \alpha}\, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: A feasible pair $(\x,t) \in \Delta \times \mathbb R$ satisfies \begin{equation}\mathbb P(\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb \x \leq t) \geq \alpha \quad  if and only if  \quad \x^{\top}\overline \Qb \x \leq t.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\mathbb P(\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb \x \leq t) \geq \alpha \quad  if and only if  \quad \x^{\top}\overline \Qb \x \leq t.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-051: Hence $\ell(\overline \Qb)$ is an epigraphic formulation of $\ell^{\rm (cce) _{\mathbb P, \alpha}$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Hence we have established a deterministic StQP equivalent of the CCE-StQP under reasonable distributional assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-053: This is in line with several robust counterparts~\cite{Bomz20a .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Now for a special case, we find an even closer connection to some special robust StQP models, namely with Frobenius balls as uncertainty set; see the following section.
elements.sent-2411-055: An appropriate model for real symmetric random matrices are Wigner matrices~\cite{wigner1958distribution with i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Wigner's semicircle law states that for off-diagonals centered at zero and and denoting by $\tau$ the second moment of the off-diagonals, the empirical spectral measure (count of eigenvalues in a certain interval), scaled by $\frac{1 {\sqrt{n }$, converges weakly to the Wigner semicircle distribution with density \begin{equation}\mu_{sc}(x):=\frac{1}{2\pi \tau^2}\sqrt{(4\tau^2-x^2)_+}\, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: The Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble (GOE) consists of Wigner matrices $\widetilde \Gb = (\widetilde g_{ij )$ such that $\widetilde g_{ii}$ are i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Perturbation of a nominal matrix by a GOE matrix has been extensively explored in the literature, see \cite{aizenman2017matrix , \cite{shapiro2016level .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: In our distributional uncertainty model, suppose that the random matrix $\widetilde \Qb$ is of the following form \begin{equation}\label{def_Q_normal} \widetilde \Qb = \Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \beta \, \widetilde \Gb\end{equation} where the real symmetric nominal matrix $\Qb^{\rm (nom)}$ is perturbed by a GOE matrix $\widetilde \Gb$ multiplied by a factor $\beta \neq 0$ which measures the degree of randomness.
elements.sent-2411-060: As an application of this model, consider portfolio optimization, where the portfolio consists of $n$ assets with historical mean returns $\r$ and historical covariance matrix $\Cb$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The goal is then to find an allocation vector $\x = (x_1,\dots,x_n)^{\top$ where $x_i$ represents the portion of the budget devoted to asset $i$ and which minimizes the expected risk and maximizes the expected return \begin{equation}\label{mean-variance} \min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}\Cb\x - \r^{\top}\x\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Note that in this model, short-selling is not allowed.
elements.sent-2411-063: By definition of the feasible set $\Delta$, problem \eqref{mean-variance is equivalent to the StQP \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}\Qb^{\rm (nom)}\x\, ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\Qb^{\rm (nom)}:= \frac{1}{2}(\Cb- \r \e^{\top} - \e \r^{\top})\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Suppose that in the estimation of the covariance matrix $\Cb$ and expected return vector $\r$ measurement errors were made so that we replace the matrix $\Qb^{\rm (nom)}$ by an additive perturbation with a GOE matrix $\widetilde \Gb$ and model the mean-variance portfolio optimization problem as an uncertain StQP \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}(\Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \beta \, \widetilde \Gb)\x\, ,\end{equation} where $\beta$ is a parameter denoting the amplitude of the perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-065: It is clear that under assumption \eqref{def_Q_normal the here-and-now problem \eqref{here-and-now_StQP consists of solving the following problem \begin{equation}\label{StQP_GOE_han} \min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \x^{\top}\Qb^{\rm (nom)}\x.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Since no assumptions on the definiteness of $\Qb^{\rm (nom)}$ are made, the here-and-now problem can be potentially indefinite.
elements.sent-2411-067: We will study the chance-constrained epigraphic StQP \eqref{StQP_cce for this choice~\eqref{def_Q_normal of $\widetilde \Qb$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: We start with the following obvious \begin{observation Let $\widetilde \Gb$ be GOE, then, for any decision vector $\x\in \mathbb R^n$, the quadratic form $\x^{\top\widetilde \Gb \x$ is a normally distributed random variable with mean 0 and variance $2||\x||_2^4$, i.e. \begin{equation}\x^{\top}\widetilde \Gb \x \sim N(0,2||\x||_2^4).\end{equation}. \end{observation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Let $\widetilde \Qb$ be defined as in \eqref{def_Q_normal , then \begin{equation}\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb\x \sim \mathcal N \left(\mu(\x), \sigma(\x)\right)\quadwith\quad \mu(\x)= \x^{\top}\Qb^{\rm (nom)}\x and \sigma(\x) = \sqrt{2}\beta\, \x^{\top}\Ib\x \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Therefore for GOE perturbations, the chance-constrained epigraphic problem \eqref{StQP_cce is equivalent to a deterministic StQP \begin{equation}\label{StQP_GOE_CCE} \ell_\alpha^{(cce)}:= \min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \x^{\top}\Qb^{\rm (cce)}\x\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\Qb^{\rm (cce)}:= \Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \sqrt{2}\beta\, \Phi^{-1}(\alpha)\,\Ib\end{equation} where $\Phi$ denotes the cumulative distribution function of a standard normal random variable.}
elements.sent-2411-072: We proceed with the announced relation of above result to robust counterparts when uncertainty sets are Frobenius balls:.
elements.sent-2411-073: Under the GOE perturbation model, define \begin{equation}\rho = \sqrt{2}\beta\, \Phi^{-1}(\alpha)\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-074: Then the CCE-StQP~\eqref{StQP_GOE_CCE can be interpreted as the deterministic counterpart of the robust StQP \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U} \,\x^{\top}( \Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \Ub)\x\end{equation} with Frobenius ball uncertainty set \begin{equation}\mathcal U = \{\Ub = \Ub^{\top}: || \Ub ||_F \leq \rho\}\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Recall that the Frobenius norm is defined by $ ||\Ub||_F^2 := \operatorname{trace (\Ub^{\top}\Ub)$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We use~\cite[Theorem 4 and Remark 5]{Bomz20a , defining $\Lb:=-\rho\,(\Cb\Cb^{\top )^{-1}$ with $\Cb=\Ib$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Flipping the sense of optimization, we obtain the deterministic counterpart of the robust problem with uncertainty set $\mathcal U$ \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U} \,\x^{\top}(\Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \Ub)\x = \min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \x^{\top}( \Qb^{\rm (nom)} +\rho\,\Ib)\x\, ,\end{equation} which is exactly~\eqref{StQP_GOE_CCE .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Observe that a similar result for shifted Wishart uncertainty models seems not to hold because of the negative shift in $\Mb = -\eta\, \Ib$.
elements.sent-2411-079: We expect that the more general distributional assumption of Property~1 would neither allow for such a characterization.
elements.sent-2411-080: We close this section discussing a possible convexifying effect by passing from the indefinite nominal StQP to the CCE-StQP.
elements.sent-2411-081: Let $\lambda_{\rm max}$ and $\lambda_{\rm min}$ denote the largest and smallest eigenvalues of the nominal matrix $\Qb^{\rm (nom)}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-082: We want to study the cases where the here-and-now problem \eqref{StQP_GOE_han is indefinite while the chance-constrained epigraphic problem \eqref{StQP_GOE_CCE is convex.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Let $\alpha > 1/2$, $\beta > 0$, $\lambda_{\rm max > 0 > \lambda_{\rm min}$ and $\Phi$ denote the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution.. Then \begin{equation}\label{condition_alpha} \Qb^{\rm (cce)} is positive-semidefinite \iff \alpha \geq \Phi\left(\frac{|\lambda_{\rm min}|}{\sqrt{2}\beta}\right) \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Note that $\Qb^{\rm (cce)}$ will never be concave but may be indefinite, namely if $\alpha < \Phi\left(\frac{|\lambda_{\rm min | {\sqrt{2}\beta}\right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: In this section we carried out a number of experiments for the model discussed in Section \ref{sec:GOE .}
elements.sent-2411-086: All results were computed using {\tt Gurobi .}

elements.sent-2411-099: To be more precise, let \begin{equation}\ell^{\rm (nom)} := \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10}\ell\left(\mathsf Q_i^{\rm (nom)}\right), \quad \quad \ell^{\rm (emp)} := \frac{1}{1000}\sum_{i=1}^{10}\sum_{j=1}^{100}\ell\left(\mathsf Q_{ij}\right),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\ell^{\rm (nom)}_{\rm cce,\alpha} := \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10}\mathbf x^{\rm (cce) \top}_{i,\alpha} \mathsf Q_i^{\rm (nom)}\mathbf x^{\rm (cce)}_{i,\alpha}, \quad \quad \ell^{\rm (emp)}_{\rm cce,\alpha} := \frac{1}{1000}\sum_{i=1}^{10}\sum_{j=1}^{100}\mathbf x^{\rm (cce) \top}_{i,\alpha} \mathsf Q_{ij}\mathbf x^{\rm (cce)}_{i,\alpha}\, .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-100: The differences are considerable, but sometimes less conservative compared to robust methods, as showcased by the following experiment where we constructed, for each scenario $i=1,\dots,10$, a robust StQP \eqref{robust_StQP each with the choice of a box uncertainty set of the form \begin{equation}\label{uncertainty_set} \mathcal U_i = \left\{\Ub = \Ub^{\top} : \rho [\underline{\Qb_{i}} -\Qb^{\rm (nom)}_i]_{k\ell}\le \Ub _{k\ell} \le \rho [\overline{\Qb_{i} }-\Qb^{\rm (nom)}_i]_{k\ell} for all k, \ell\right\}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}(\underline{\Qb_{i}})_{k\ell} := \min_{j} \, (\Qb_{ij})_{k\ell}, \quad \quad (\overline{\Qb_{i}})_{k\ell} := \max_{j} \, (\Qb_{ij})_{k\ell}\end{equation} defines a box covering all realizations (depending on the instance number $i$), and $\rho\in (0,1]$ is a parameter controlling the size of $\mathcal U_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-101: By application of~\cite[Theorem~3]{Bomz20a to the constructed uncertainty sets $\mathcal U_i$, we obtain \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U_i} \,\x^{\top}(\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)} + \Ub)\x = \min_{\x \in \Delta} \left [\x^{\top}\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)}\x + \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U_i} \,\x^{\top}\Ub\x\right ] =\left [ \min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)}\x + \rho\,\x^{\top}( \overline{\Qb_{i}} -\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)} )\x\right ] = \min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}[(1-\rho)\,\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)} + \rho \,\overline{\Qb_{i}}]\x\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-102: We set $\rho = 0.8$ and denote by $\x_{i ^{\rm (rob)}$ the robust solution obtained from the robust StQPs \eqref{robust_StQP with uncertainty sets \eqref{uncertainty_set .}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: We compare the obtained realized feasible values in a similar way as above, this time observing the differences $\ell^{\rm (nom) _{\rm rob -\ell^{\rm (nom)}$ and $\ell^{\rm (emp) _{\rm rob -\ell^{\rm (emp)}$ with \begin{equation}\ell^{\rm (nom)}_{\rm rob } := \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10}\mathbf x_{i}^{\rm (rob)\top}\mathsf Q_i^{\rm (nom)}\mathbf x_{i}^{\rm (rob)} \quad and\quad \ell^{\rm (emp)}_{\rm rob} := \frac{1}{1000}\sum_{i=1}^{10}\sum_{j=1}^{100}\mathbf x_{i}^{\rm (rob)\top} \mathsf Q_{ij}\mathbf x_{i}^{\rm (rob)} \, .\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.figure-2411-002: [H] \begin{minipage}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{FIG-33-avg-diff-cce-rob.png} \caption{$\ell^{\rm (nom)}_{\rm cce,\alpha}-\ell^{\rm (nom)}$ vs $ \ell^{\rm (nom)}_{\rm rob }-\ell^{\rm (nom)}$}\label{fig:5} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{FIG-44-avg-diff-cce-rob.png} \caption{$\ell^{\rm (emp)}_{\rm cce,\alpha}-\ell^{\rm (emp)}$ vs $\ell^{\rm (emp)}_{\rm rob }-\ell^{\rm (emp)}$}\label{fig:6} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-104: We introduced and motivated the chance-constrained epigraphic StQP, a new model for solving uncertain StQPs under distributional assumptions, and established a deterministic counterpart as an instance in the same problem class (another StQP).
elements.sent-2411-105: Our findings parallel similar observations on robust StQPs, and indeed a special variant of this model is equivalent to a particular robust formulation.
elements.sent-2411-106: However, preliminary experiments seem to suggest that the chance-constrained epigraphic StQP can be less conservative than a robust approach, if the confidence level (probabilistic optimality guarantee) is not too large.
elements.sent-2411-107: Thanks are due to Christa Cuchiero and Abdel Lisser for stimulating discussions and valuable suggestions.
elements.sent-2411-108: Both authors are indebted to VGSCO for financial support enabling presentation of this work at various major conferences: EurOpt 2024, EURO 2024 and ISMP 2024.
elements.sent-2411-109: We profited from the feedback of delegates to these meetings, which we hope may lead to future extensions of our findings.. .
elements.sent-2411-110: Keywords: Stochastic optimization, Quadratic optimization, Chance constraints, Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble\newline MSC(2020) Classification: 90C20, 90C15, 90C26.
elements.sent-2411-111: The standard quadratic optimization problem (StQP) consists of minimizing a quadratic form over the standard simplex \begin{equation}\ell(\Qb):=\min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}\Qb\x {\color{black}\, ,}\end{equation} where $\Qb \in \mathbb{R ^{n\times n}$ is a symmetric matrix, and $\Delta:= \{\x \in \mathbb{R ^n: \e^{\top}\x = 1, \x \geq \boldsymbol{0}\}$ is the standard simplex in $\mathbb{R ^n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Here $\e\in \mathbb{R ^n$ is the vector of all ones and ${ ^{\top}$ denotes transposition; $\Ib_n:= {\rm Diag (\e)$ denotes the $n\times n$ identity matrix.}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Even though the StQP is simple - minimization of a quadratic function under linear constraints - it is NP-hard without assumptions on the definiteness of the matrix $\Qb${\color{black .}}
elements.sent-2411-114: O bserve that convex, but also concave instances are polynomially solvable, the latter even in closed form: $\ell(\Qb) = \min_{i \Qb_{ii}$.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Despite of its continuous optimization nature, it also serves to model discrete problems like the maximum-clique problem as well, as witnessed by above references.
elements.sent-2411-116: One of the most natural ways to deal with uncertain objective functions {\color{black is via robust optimization \cite{ben2009robust .}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: In that paradigm, the decision-maker has to decide upon an uncertainty set $\mathcal U$ which encapsulates all the known information about the uncertain parameter.
elements.sent-2411-118: The uncertain parameter (in this case the uncertain data matrix $\Qb$) is suppossed to reside within the uncertainty set $\mathcal U$, as violations are not allowed \cite{gorissen2015practical .}
elements.sent-2411-119: Bomze et al.~\cite{Bomz20a introduced the concept of a robust standard quadratic optimization problem, which they formulated as a minimax problem \begin{equation}\label{robust_StQP} \min_{\x \in \Delta} \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U} \,\x^{\top}(\Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \Ub)\x\end{equation} with uncertainty set $\mathcal U$.}
elements.sent-2411-120: As opposed to \cite{bomze2022two we will assume that the full data matrix $\widetilde \Qb$ is random according to a known distribution $\mathbb P$.}
elements.section-2411-007: Chance-constrained epigraphic models of random StQPs
elements.sent-2411-121: Then the CCEStQP is defined by the problem \begin{equation}\label{StQP_cce}\tag{CCEStQP} \begin{array}{lcl} \ell^{\rm (cce)}_{\mathbb P, \alpha} := \underset{\x,t}{\min} t \text{s.t.} \mathbb P[\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb\x \leq t] \geq \alpha \x \in \Delta\, ,\, t\in \mathbb{R}\, . \end{array}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-041: \begin{equation}\label{StQP_cce}\tag{CCEStQP} {lcl} \ell^{\rm (cce)}_{\mathbb P, \alpha} := \underset{\x,t}{\min} t s.t. \mathbb P[\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb\x \leq t] \geq \alpha \x \in \Delta\, ,\, t\in R\, . \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-122: The reason for the name of CCEStQP can be explained as follows.
elements.sent-2411-123: It is well known that any optimization problem \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \mathcal{X}} \, f(\x)\end{equation} can be rewritten equivalently in epigraphic form by introducing an extra variable $t$ \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\underset{\x,t}{\min} \quad t \nonumber s.t. \quad f(\x) \leq t \label{soft_constraint} \quad \x \in X, \, t\in R. \label{hard_constraint}\end{equation} \end{subequations Now suppose that $f:\mathbb R^n \times \mathbb R^m \to \mathbb R$ is a random objective function that depends on the decision variable $\x \in \mathcal X \subseteq \mathbb R^n$ and on the random vector $\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi \in \Xi \subseteq \mathbb R^m$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Then we can regard constraint \eqref{soft_constraint as a soft constraint and request feasibility with probability of at least $\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-125: This has a familiar interpretation familiar in risk management as the so-called Value-at-Risk (VaR) of $f(\x,\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi )$ at $\x$ with confidence level $\alpha \in (0,1)$, defined as~\cite{hull2012risk \begin{equation}\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(\x):=\min \, \{ t: \mathbb P[f(\x,\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi}) \leq t] \geq \alpha\}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: The problem of minimizing VaR~\cite{Larsen2002 is \begin{equation}\label{prob_min_var} \begin{array}{lcl} \underset{\x \in \mathcal X}{\min}\,\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(\x) = \underset{\x,t}{\min} t \text{s.t.} \mathbb P[f(\x,\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi}) \leq t] \geq \alpha \x \in \mathcal X, \, t\in \mathbb{R}\, . \end{array}\end{equation} El Ghaoui et al.~\cite{ghaoui2003worst investigated problem~\eqref{prob_min_var in the context of worst-case VaR for portfolio optimization with a return function \begin{equation}f(\x,\tilde \xi) := \x^{\top}\tilde \xi\end{equation} where $\boldsymbol{\tilde \xi}$ was a vector of returns.}}}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Distributional models for random (indefinite) symmetric matrices
elements.sent-2411-127: We are interested in instances of \eqref{StQP_cce where the random matrix $\widetilde \Qb$ is indefinite.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Generate a random $n\times n$ matrix $\widetilde \Rb$ and put $\widetilde\Qb :=\Qb^{\rm (nom) + \frac{\beta {\sqrt{2 (\widetilde \Rb+\widetilde\Rb^{\top )$, for some nominal matrix $\Qb^{\rm (nom)}$ and a suitable parameter $\beta$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Generate a random $n\times p$ matrix $\widetilde \Yb$ and put $\widetilde\Qb :=\widetilde\Yb\widetilde\Yb^{\top - \eta\,\Ib_n$.}
elements.sent-2411-130: The parameter $\eta >0$ has to be large enough such that the positive semi-definite matrix $\widetilde\Yb\widetilde\Yb^{\top}$ becomes indefinite after subtraction of $\eta\,\Ib_n$, but not too large to avoid negative-definite matrices $\widetilde \Qb$.
elements.sent-2411-131: In case that $\widetilde \Rb$ or $\widetilde \Yb$ are normal with independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) entries we obtain the shifted and scaled Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble in the first case and the shifted Wishart Ensemble in the second case~\cite{geman1980limit,marchenko1967distribution,silverstein1985smallest .}
elements.sent-2411-132: Let $\widetilde \Gb = (\widetilde g_{ij )$ be a random symmetric matrix such that $\widetilde g_{ii}$ are i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Let $\Qb^{\rm (nom) \in \mathbb R^{n\times n}$ be a nominal matrix, $\beta \neq 0$ and $\widetilde \Gb$ be a GOE matrix, then the GOE perturbation model is defined as \begin{equation}\label{def_Q_normal} \widetilde \Qb = \Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \beta \, \widetilde \Gb\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Let $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\in \mathbb R^{n\times n}$ be a positive-definite matrix and \sloppy$\widetilde\y_1,\dots,\widetilde\y_p\sim \mathcal N_n(\boldsymbol{0 ,\boldsymbol{\Sigma )$ be i.i.d.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Set $\widetilde \Yb = (\widetilde\y_1,\dots,\widetilde\y_p)$, then the matrix $\widetilde \Wb = \widetilde \Yb \widetilde \Yb^{\top}$ is said to have a Wishart distribution with covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ and $p$ degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-136: Let $\widetilde \Wb$ be a Wishart matrix and $\eta > 0$, then the shifted Wishart model is defined as \begin{equation}\label{def_Q_wishart} \widetilde \Qb = \widetilde \Wb - \eta \, \Ib_n\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-137: For these distributions, we derive the following location-scale property of the distributions of the quadratic forms $\x\T\widetilde \Qb\x$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Obviously, for any decision vector $\x\in \mathbb R^n$, the quadratic form $\x^{\top}\widetilde \Gb \x$ is a normally distributed random variable with mean 0 and variance $2||\x||_2^4$, i.e. \begin{equation}\x^{\top}\widetilde \Gb \x \sim N(0,2||\x||_2^4)\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-139: Now we turn to the shifted Wishart model \eqref{def_Q_wishart .}
elements.sent-2411-140: We have that $\x^{\top}\widetilde\Wb\x$ is a gamma distributed random variable with shape $k= p/2$ and scale $\theta= 2\x^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\x$, where $p$ determines the shape of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-141: A GOE perturbation model \eqref{def_Q_normal has several applications, among them portfolio optimization, where the portfolio consists of $n$ assets with historical mean returns $\r$ and historical covariance matrix $\Cb$.}
elements.sent-2411-142: In particular, the entry $x_i$ represents the portion of the budget devoted to the $i$-th asset.
elements.sent-2411-143: The goal is to find an allocation vector $\x$} which minimizes the expected risk and maximizes the expected return \begin{equation}\label{mean-variance} \min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}\Cb\x - \r^{\top}\x\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-144: By definition of the feasible set $\Delta$, problem \eqref{mean-variance is equivalent to the StQP \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}\Qb^{\rm (nom)}\x\, ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\Qb^{\rm (nom)}:= \tfrac{1}{2}(\Cb- \r \e^{\top} - \e \r^{\top})\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Since no assumptions on the definiteness of $\Qb^{\rm (nom)}$ are made, the here-and-now problem \eqref{StQP_GOE_han can be potentially indefinite.}
elements.section-2411-008: A distributional assumption and main results
elements.subsection-2411-003: A reasonable distributional assumption on random StQPs
elements.sent-2411-146: Then the chance-constrained epigraphic StQP~\eqref{StQP_cce is equivalent to a deterministic StQP.}
elements.sent-2411-148: A feasible pair $(\x,t) \in \Delta \times \mathbb R$ satisfies \begin{equation}\mathbb P[\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb \x \leq t] \geq \alpha \quad  if and only if  \quad \x^{\top}\overline \Qb \x \leq t.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-047: \begin{equation}\mathbb P[\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb \x \leq t] \geq \alpha \quad  if and only if  \quad \x^{\top}\overline \Qb \x \leq t.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-149: Therefore $\eqref{StQP_cce}$ is equivalent to \begin{equation}\begin{array}{cl} \underset{\x,t}{\min} t \text{s.t.} \mathbb \x^{\top}\overline \Qb\x \leq t \x \in \Delta\, ,\, t\in \mathbb{R}\,, \end{array}\end{equation} which is precisely the epigraphic reformulation of the deterministic StQP \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta}\, \x^{\top}\overline{\Qb}\x\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-150: Hence we have established a deterministic StQP equivalent to the CCEStQP under reasonable distributional assumptions.
elements.subsection-2411-005: An important special case: GOE perturbation model
elements.sent-2411-151: Let $\widetilde \Qb$ be defined as in \eqref{def_Q_normal , then \begin{equation}\x^{\top}\widetilde \Qb\x \sim \mathcal N \left(\mu(\x), \sigma^2(\x)\right)\quadwith\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mu(\x)= \x^{\top}\Qb^{\rm (nom)}\x and \sigma(\x) = \sqrt{2}\beta\, \x^{\top}\Ib_n\x \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Therefore for the GOE perturbation model, the chance-constrained epigraphic problem \eqref{StQP_cce is equivalent to a deterministic StQP \begin{equation}\label{StQP_GOE_CCE} \ell_\alpha^{(cce)}:= \min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \x^{\top}\Qb^{\rm (cce)}\x\,,\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\Qb^{\rm (cce)}:= \Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \sqrt{2}\beta\, \Phi^{-1}(\alpha)\,\Ib_n\end{equation} where $\Phi$ denotes the cumulative distribution function of a standard normal random variable.}
elements.sent-2411-154: We proceed with the announced relation of above result to robust counterparts when uncertainty sets are Frobenius balls {\color{black under the GOE perturbation model:.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Under the GOE perturbation model \eqref{def_Q_normal , define \begin{equation}\rho := \sqrt{2}\beta\, \Phi^{-1}(\alpha)\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Then the CCEStQP~\eqref{StQP_GOE_CCE can be interpreted as the deterministic counterpart of the robust StQP \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U} \,\x^{\top}( \Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \Ub)\x\,,\end{equation} with Frobenius ball uncertainty set \begin{equation}\mathcal U = \{\Ub = \Ub^{\top}: || \Ub ||_F \leq \rho\}\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-157: We use~\cite[Theorem 4 and Remark 5]{Bomz20a , defining $\Lb:=-\rho\,(\Cb\Cb^{\top )^{-1}$ with $\Cb=\Ib_n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Flipping the sense of optimization, we obtain the deterministic counterpart of the robust problem with uncertainty set $\mathcal U$ \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U} \,\x^{\top}(\Qb^{\rm (nom)} + \Ub)\x = \min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \x^{\top}( \Qb^{\rm (nom)} +\rho\,\Ib_n)\x\, ,\end{equation} which is exactly~\eqref{StQP_GOE_CCE .}
elements.sent-2411-159: Observe that a similar result for the shifted Wishart model seems not to hold because of the negative shift in $\Mb = -\eta\, \Ib_n$.

elements.sent-2411-178: In the following experiment inspired by~\cite{Bomz20a , we constructed, for each scenario $i=1,\dots,10$, a robust StQP \eqref{robust_StQP each with the choice of a box uncertainty set of the form \begin{equation}\label{uncertainty_set} \mathcal U_i = \left\{\Ub = \Ub^{\top} : \rho (\underline{\Qb_{i}} -\Qb^{\rm (nom)}_i) \leq \Ub \leq \rho (\overline{\Qb_{i} }-\Qb^{\rm (nom)}_i)\right\} \,,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}(\underline{\Qb_{i}})_{k\ell} := \min_{j} \, (\Qb_{ij})_{k\ell}, \quad \quad (\overline{\Qb_{i}})_{k\ell} := \max_{j} \, (\Qb_{ij})_{k\ell}\end{equation} defines a box covering all realizations (depending on the instance number $i$), and $\rho\in (0,1]$ is a parameter controlling the size of $\mathcal U_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: By application of~\cite[Theorem~3]{Bomz20a to the constructed uncertainty sets $\mathcal U_i$, we obtain \begin{equation}\min_{\x \in \Delta} \, \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U_i} \,\x^{\top}(\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)} + \Ub)\x = \min_{\x \in \Delta} \left [\x^{\top}\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)}\x + \max_{\Ub \in \mathcal U_i} \,\x^{\top}\Ub\x\right ] = \min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)}\x + \rho\,\x^{\top}( \overline{\Qb_{i}} -\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)} )\x = \min_{\x \in \Delta} \,\x^{\top}[(1-\rho)\,\Qb_i^{\rm (nom)} + \rho \,\overline{\Qb_{i}}]\x\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Figure \ref{fig:3 depicts the (average) absolute error of the solutions $\mathbf x^{\rm (cce) _{i,\alpha}$ and $\mathbf x^{\rm (rob) _{i}$ with respect to the optimal solutions of $\ell(\mathsf Q_i^{\rm (nom) )$, i.e. the differences $|\ell^{\rm (nom) _{\rm cce,\alpha - \ell^{\rm (nom) |$ as a solid line and $|\ell^{\rm (nom) _{\rm rob - \ell^{\rm (nom) |$ as a dashdotted line and Figure \ref{fig:4 presents the (average) absolute error of the solutions $\mathbf x^{\rm (cce) _{i,\alpha}$ and $\mathbf x^{\rm (rob) _{i}$ with respect to the optimal solutions of $\ell(\mathsf Q_{ij )$, i.e. the differences $|\ell^{\rm (emp) _{\rm cce,\alpha - \ell^{\rm (emp) |$ as a solid line and $|\ell^{\rm (emp) _{\rm rob - \ell^{\rm (emp) |$ as a dashdotted line.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-004: [H] \begin{minipage}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{FIG-33-AE-nom-cce-rob.png} \caption{$|\ell^{\rm (nom)}_{\rm cce,\alpha} - \ell^{\rm (nom)}|$ vs $|\ell^{\rm (nom)}_{\rm rob } - \ell^{\rm (nom)}|$} \label{fig:3} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{FIG-44-AE-emp-cce-rob.png} \caption{$|\ell^{\rm (emp)}_{\rm cce,\alpha}-\ell^{\rm (emp)}|$ vs $|\ell^{\rm (emp)}_{\rm rob}-\ell^{\rm (emp)}|$} \label{fig:4} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-181: As one can see in Figure \ref{fig:3 , for the nominal problem $\ell(\Qb_i^{\rm (nom) )$ the chance-constrained epigraphic solutions $\mathbf x^{\rm (cce) _{i,\alpha}$ are less conservative than the robust solutions $\mathbf x^{\rm (rob) _{i}$ for all $\alpha \leq 0.72$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: For the empirical problem $\ell(\Qb_{ij )$, Figure \ref{fig:4 shows that any $\alpha \leq 0.79$ yields less conservative chance-constrained epigraphic solutions than the robust solutions.}}
elements.sent-2411-183: The authors thank Christa Cuchiero and Abdel Lisser for stimulating discussions and valuable suggestions.
elements.sent-2411-184: The authors profited from the feedback of delegates to these meetings, and hope that it may lead to future extensions of the present findings.
